title: Are Markets Underpricing Sudden Stop Risk?

some big warning lights are flashing.
That is according to our next guest. Uh
joining us now is founder and CEO Darius
Dale. He's with 42 Macro. Uh Darius,
thanks for your time.
Thank you, Mayra. It's great to be here.
Appreciate you. What's the warning sign
you're seeing?
Well, the warning sign is very clearly
the fact that the Strait of Hormuz
remains closed. I don't think I'm
breaking glass when I suggest that. Uh
there's very clearly significant
negative ramifications for the global
liquidity cycle should the global supply
chain cycle continue to be
uh offline or at least in severely
impaired.
And we this has been going on, of
course, for over a month and a half. Are
you beginning to feel like there are
going to be much longer-term effects
than perhaps the market uh seems to to
buy into?
Uh no, not yet. Uh we're still in a
risk-on market regime. Uh I would feel
that way if we transition back to a
risk-off market regime. In our opinion,
what's likely to catalyze that is a
breaking of the ceasefire. Right now,
the markets are giving the US
administration and the Iranian
administration the benefit of the doubt
as it relates to the negotiations and
the brinksmanship. However, if we return
back to kinetic warfare, that that sort
of area under the left side of the the
distribution of probable economic policy
and market outcomes that was removed
when we announced when they announced
the ceasefire on April 7th, we would
have to put that that area back there
under that curve, which will ultimately
cause uh market outcomes to get
significantly worse uh than they
currently are priced. Okay, so what is
your timeline to see uh see a change in
the markets? And I'm asking because we
all know the US president came out early
and said, "Oh, this will be days. Oh,
okay, it'll be maybe a week or two." And
that timeline keeps getting extended. At
what point in that timeline do you start
to worry about longer-term weakness in
the market?
Yeah, I mean, look, it's it's it's the
market's job to determine that, not
mine. I I I don't think anybody has an
appropriate uh uh forecast on that. Uh
the one thing I will say, it really
behooves us as investors to have
high-quality risk management signals
because this is the the degree of sudden
stop risk of that is currently embedded
in the global financial markets and the
global economy, in my opinion, is as
high as it's been since we were in the
since about January of 2020 heading into
COVID. Uh this is the last time we've
seen such a divergence between market
prices and what could ultimately happen
from a a left tail distribution
standpoint from an economic and and a
liquidity standpoint. And so,
ultimately, it's you you're going to
need some high-quality risk management
signals to get you out to the extent
that these ceasefire uh discussions uh
devolve more materially. Okay, let's
talk a little bit about uh tech, which
has had a resurgence in the past couple
of weeks and AI build-out, which uh we
know over the last sort of 6 months of
2025 and the start of this year,
CAPEX was a big concern for investors.
They wanted to see some return on on
revenue. When you look at what's
happening now and that historic
build-out, what time in history can you
compare it to?
Uh
investors are not going to like this,
but it it it very much reminds us of, in
terms of our research process, of the
work we did on the uh railroad uh CAPEX
uh cycle back in the 1850s to 1870s, the
consumer durable goods CAPEX cycles in
the 1920s, as well as the internet
technology CAPEX cycle in the 1990s. And
the thing that all three of those things
have in common of those three CAPEX
cycles have in common is that they were
uh uh they were followed by secular bear
markets. And so, it's our belief that
we're going to have a secular bear
market at the end of this uh bull market
run, but in our view, based on our our
our fundamental uh research on the other
five key macro cycles of the the outside
of the positioning cycle, it's our view
that the bull market continues uh at
least over the medium term, but at some
point when we peak, uh that's probably
going to be be it for a while for for
quite a long while. Okay, let's talk
about sectors that you like right now.
Where are you putting money?
Yeah, sectors that we like. So, in our
in our opinion, assuming that we get a
we get to eat to get a reasonably
positive resolution uh to the US-Iran uh
dynamic, uh then it's likely that our
source of funds theme will go back on.
Right now, it's off. We've been we've
been wrong on that theme in terms of
investors flowing back into uh the
perceived safety and the earnings
durability of the mega-cap tech trade.
But in our opinion, if AI is
appropriately diffused throughout the
economy, which we believe it to be, then
what's likely to happen is that we're
going to see a convergence in the
profitability uh and the productivity of
of sectors and factors that have been
left behind. So, from our perspective,
if you pull up a 10-year chart of
anything, you can very clearly see that
international stocks ex-US, emerging
market stocks, uh cyclicals, small caps,
these are all factors that have been
left for dead because the tech sector
was demonstrably outperforming them from
a a revenue, margin, cash flow
generation perspective, but that's
likely to converge over time as AI
diffuses throughout those business
models. Okay, so are there sectors
you're staying away from?
Uh do you mean in during a risk-on
market regime, you're naturally going to
want to be staying away from low beta
sectors like utilities, consumer
staples, and and and uh healthcare, uh
and real estate. And those are the
things that it's not that there's
anything wrong with those sectors cuz
they'll go up if the market goes up, but
the reality is you tend to want to be
long high beta cyclicals as a factor uh
when you're in a risk-on market regime
as we currently are. Okay, we talked a
little bit about the Iran conflict and
what it means or could mean down the
line. What about on the inflation front?
How much of a hit do you think fuel
prices will be on inflation uh in the
next part of the year?
Yeah, we already saw it. I mean, look,
the the rate of change of energy prices
have to continue to rise uh for it to
have an incremental impact on inflation.
Uh so, in our opinion, uh we we look at
our our our fundamental forecasting, you
know, we have three themes that are
currently weighing on inflation from a
core inflation standpoint, which is much
more what the Fed is going to be focused
on and other central banks around the
world, starting with our productivity
boom theme, our jobs recovery theme, and
our cooling housing and labor theme.
These are some pretty big weights uh in
the core CPI basket that should continue
to be an anchor on core inflation
statistics and and the kinds of
statistics that Kevin Warsh were
highlighting uh a couple days ago, like
trim mean PCE, trim mean CPI. And so,
from our perspective, from our
perspective, we think there's a
tremendous amount of downside surprise
risk on inflation relative to consensus
over the medium term, just as we believe
there's a tremendous amount of upside
surprise risk relative to consensus for
growth, and that's a really positive
backdrop for markets assuming we can get
a positive resolution to the conflict in
the Middle East.
Darius Dale, founder CEO 42 Macro.
Thanks for your time. Appreciate it.
Thank you. Appreciate you having me.
